text:
In this work, we introduce Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a new prompting framework that significantly improves the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs) by representing their thinking process as a graph. Building on the success of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Tree of Thoughts (ToT), current LLMs are constrained to execute their operations in a sequence or a tree. In contrast, the proposed Graph of Thoughts allows for richer thought transformations such as (i) combining multiple reasoning flows; (ii) employing feedback loops to recursively update and refine the outputs; and (iii) connecting separate knowledge chunks (graph vertices) conditioned on their associated logical edges.

In GoT, we introduced a new modular architecture for models by breaking them down into a Controller, Prompter, and Parser to act as a conduit for “thinking” and for breaking down and reassembling complex ideas. When we evaluate GoT on several different benchmarks including sorting, set operations, and document merging, we show that it outperforms other current prompting methods by a large margin. For example, our sorting benchmark shows that GoT improves output quality by 62% over ToT while reducing computation time by more than 31%. We also introduced a new measure of the structural properties of models that we call “thought volume” and showed how this is maximized in graph-based models that spread out the output of large language models (LLMs) to maximize information gain, and thus bringing LLM prompting closer to the highly non-linear, networked process of human and algorithmic thinking.
explanation: The text uses highly polished, buzzword-heavy wording with novel terms (Graph of Thoughts, thought volume) and precise benchmark claims, which is characteristic of AI-generated abstracts, though a careful human author could produce it as well.
